The witnesses agree the verse reveals God's sovereign ordering of all the nations with his own people in view: "the Most High divided to the nations their inheritance... set the bounds... according to the children of Israel"; in apportioning the earth among the peoples, God had Israel in his eye, ordering all for the sake of his elect; it teaches that the whole history and geography of the nations is under God's disposal and bent toward his redemptive purpose, "he hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation" (Acts 17:26), all the nations ordered with his people in mind.

The witnesses agree the verse sets forth the surpassing tenderness of God's care: he "found" Israel in the desolate wilderness, "led," "instructed," and "kept him as the apple of his eye" — the most precious and guarded part of the body; the figures heap up the gentleness and watchfulness of his love, finding the helpless and tending them as a most cherished treasure; it teaches the intimate care of God for his own, who guards them as that which is dearest, "he that toucheth you toucheth the apple of his eye" (Zech. 2:8), his people kept with a jealous tenderness.

11:
"As an eagle stirreth up her nest, fluttereth over her young, spreadeth abroad her wings, taketh them, beareth them on her wings:" The witnesses note God's care likened to the eagle stirring her nest and bearing her young on her wings. The witnesses note that Gill, with Jarchi, notes the eagle is merciful to its young, not coming suddenly but rousing them with a noise and her wings, that they be fitter to receive her. The witnesses note that she bears them on her wings. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the stirreth up her nest — rousing the young; the fluttereth over — tender watchfulness; the beareth on her wings — carrying them up; the care and discipline — borne on eagles' wings (Exod. 19:4).

The witnesses agree the eagle is a most fitting figure of God's training and bearing care: as the eagle "stirreth up her nest" to rouse her young to flight, "fluttereth over them," and "beareth them on her wings," so God roused Israel from ease, taught them to mount, and carried them; the figure unites tenderness with a wise discipline that will not leave them in the nest; it teaches that God both bears his people up and stirs them to grow, sometimes unsettling their rest for their good, "I bare you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto myself" (Exod. 19:4), his care both gentle and forming.

51:
"Because ye trespassed against me among the children of Israel at the waters of Meribah-Kadesh, in the wilderness of Zin; because ye sanctified me not in the midst of the children of Israel." The witnesses note the reason Moses was barred from the land, the trespass at Meribah — the crux of the holiness of God that even his greatest servant must honour.
